questions.question: Suppose L = {p, q, r, s, t} is a lattice represented by the following Hasse diagram (t is the top element, p is the bottom element, and q, r, s are three incomparable elements each lying between p and t). For any x, y in L, not necessarily distinct, x v y and x ^ y are the join and meet of x, y respectively. Let L^3 = {(x, y, z): x, y, z in L} be the set of all ordered triplets of the elements of L. Let p_r be the probability that an element (x, y, z) in L^3 chosen equiprobably satisfies x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z). Then A. p_r = 0 B. p_r = 1 C. 0 < p_r <= 1/5 D. 1/5 < p_r < 1
questions.to_find: The probability p_r that a random ordered triplet from this lattice satisfies the distributive law

understand.plain: The Hasse diagram is the diamond M3: one bottom p, one top t, and three mutually incomparable middle elements q, r, s, each sitting directly between p and t. This is the textbook NON-distributive lattice, so the distributive law x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z) is NOT an identity here. That kills option B (p_r = 1). But the law still holds for huge numbers of triplets (for example whenever any of x, y, z equals p or t, or whenever two of them are equal), so it is far from never true, killing option A (p_r = 0) and the tiny-bound option C. The fraction of the 5^3 = 125 triplets that satisfy the law lands strictly between 1/5 and 1, so the answer is D.

keywords.explain: A 5-element lattice with a least element, a greatest element, and three pairwise-incomparable atoms in the middle; it is the smallest non-distributive lattice.
keywords.example: Here meet of any two middle elements is p and their join is t, e.g. q ^ r = p and q v r = t.

keywords.explain: The identity x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z) (and its dual). A lattice is distributive only if this holds for every triple; M3 violates it.
keywords.example: Take x = q, y = r, z = s: q v (r ^ s) = q v p = q, but (q v r) ^ (q v s) = t ^ t = t, and q != t.

keywords.explain: Each of the 5^3 = 125 ordered triples is equally likely; p_r is just (favourable triples)/125.
keywords.example: Triples touching p or t, or with a repeat, are heavily favourable, so p_r is a sizeable fraction but not all of them.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 320' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='358' height='318' class='bg'/><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='55' x2='70' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='55' x2='180' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='55' x2='290' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='70' y1='160' x2='180' y2='265' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='160' x2='180' y2='265' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='290' y1='160' x2='180' y2='265' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='180' cy='55' r='9' class='hi'/><circle cx='70' cy='160' r='9' class='bg'/><circle cx='180' cy='160' r='9' class='bg'/><circle cx='290' cy='160' r='9' class='bg'/><circle cx='180' cy='265' r='9' class='hi'/><text x='174' y='42' class='lbl ink'>t</text><text x='48' y='166' class='lbl ink'>q</text><text x='192' y='156' class='lbl ink'>r</text><text x='302' y='166' class='lbl ink'>s</text><text x='174' y='295' class='lbl ink'>p</text><text x='60' y='312' class='tag no'>non-distributive: p_r in (1/5, 1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The diamond M3 lattice: bottom p, top t, and three incomparable middle elements q, r, s, the smallest non-distributive lattice, so the distributive law holds for some but not all triples.

given.aim: Find where the probability p_r of the distributive identity holding over all 125 random triplets falls among the four options.

terms.meaning: A 5-element lattice shaped as the diamond M3 (p bottom, t top, q/r/s incomparable atoms)
terms.example: q ^ r = p, q v r = t
terms.connects: Its non-distributive shape decides whether the identity is universal

terms.meaning: Join (least upper bound) and meet (greatest lower bound) of x and y
terms.example: q v s = t, q ^ s = p
terms.connects: The two operations appearing on both sides of the distributive law

terms.meaning: All ordered triples (x, y, z); there are 5^3 = 125 of them, each equally likely
terms.example: (q, r, s) is one such triple
terms.connects: The sample space over which p_r is computed

terms.meaning: Probability that a random triple satisfies x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z)

given.plan: Show the lattice is M3 (non-distributive) so the identity is not universal (p_r != 1), then note that the law still holds for very many triples (those involving p, t, repeats), so p_r is neither 0 nor a tiny number; conclude 1/5 < p_r < 1.

steps.title: Identify the lattice as the non-distributive M3
steps.formula_raw: x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z)  (must hold for ALL triples in a distributive lattice)
steps.apply: The three middle atoms q, r, s are pairwise incomparable with meet p and join t. Test x = q, y = r, z = s: LHS = q v (r ^ s) = q v p = q; RHS = (q v r) ^ (q v s) = t ^ t = t. Since q != t the identity FAILS for this triple, so L is not distributive and therefore p_r != 1, eliminating option B.
steps.note: M3 is the standard 5-element non-distributive lattice; one failing triple already rules out p_r = 1.

steps.title: Show the identity holds for many triples, so p_r is a large fraction
steps.formula_raw: if any of x, y, z is the bottom p or top t, or two of them coincide, the distributive identity holds
steps.apply: Whenever x = t the identity reads t = t ^ t = t (true); whenever x = p it reduces to y ^ z = y ^ z (true); equalities among x, y, z also make both sides agree. Such triples number in the dozens, so the count of favourable triples among the 125 is well above 25 (= 1/5 of 125) yet below 125. Hence p_r is strictly between 1/5 and 1, giving option D. (Counting carefully gives the favourable fraction comfortably inside this band.)
steps.note: Because failures occur only for special configurations of the three incomparable atoms, p_r stays well above 1/5 but cannot reach 1.

solution.result: L is the non-distributive M3 lattice, so the distributive identity holds for most but not all of the 125 triplets, placing p_r strictly in (1/5, 1): option D.

questions.formula_note: The key fact is that M3 is non-distributive, so x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z) fails for at least one triple (p_r != 1) yet holds for the many triples involving p, t or repeats (p_r not tiny), forcing 1/5 < p_r < 1 (D).

questions.question: Consider the set X = {a, b, c, d, e} under partial ordering R = {(a,a), (a,b), (a,c), (a,d), (a,e), (b,b), (b,c), (b,e), (c,c), (c,e), (d,d), (d,e), (e,e)}. The Hasse diagram of the partial order (X, R) is shown below (a is the bottom element; a is covered by b and d; b is covered by c; c and d are each covered by the top element e). The minimum number of ordered pairs that need to be added to R to make (X, R) a lattice is ______.
questions.to_find: How many ordered pairs must be added to R so that (X, R) becomes a lattice

understand.plain: A poset is a lattice when every pair of elements has a unique least upper bound (join) and a unique greatest lower bound (meet). Read the Hasse diagram: a is the single bottom (below everything) and e is the single top (above everything), with chains a < b < c < e and a < d < e. The only genuinely incomparable pairs are {b, d}, {c, d}, and pairs already settled by the chains. Checking those incomparable pairs, each one has a unique meet (a) and a unique join (e). So every pair already has a unique LUB and GLB: the structure is ALREADY a lattice and nothing needs to be added. The answer is 0.

keywords.explain: A partially ordered set in which every two elements have a unique least upper bound (join) and a unique greatest lower bound (meet).
keywords.example: In this poset b v d = e (unique) and b ^ d = a (unique).

keywords.explain: A least element below all others and a greatest element above all others; having both means every pair automatically has at least one upper and one lower bound.
keywords.example: Here a is the bottom (0) and e is the top (1).

keywords.explain: Two elements with neither below the other; a lattice needs each such pair to still have a unique join and meet.
keywords.example: {b, d} and {c, d} are the incomparable pairs to check.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 340' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='358' height='338' class='bg'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='280' x2='110' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='200' x2='110' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='120' x2='200' y2='55' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='280' x2='250' y2='160' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='250' y1='160' x2='200' y2='55' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='200' cy='55' r='9' class='hi'/><circle cx='110' cy='120' r='9' class='bg'/><circle cx='110' cy='200' r='9' class='bg'/><circle cx='250' cy='160' r='9' class='bg'/><circle cx='110' cy='280' r='9' class='hi'/><text x='208' y='50' class='lbl ink'>e (top)</text><text x='86' y='118' class='lbl ink'>c</text><text x='86' y='205' class='lbl ink'>b</text><text x='262' y='165' class='lbl ink'>d</text><text x='86' y='300' class='lbl ink'>a (bottom)</text><text x='30' y='328' class='tag ok'>unique join e and meet a everywhere: already a lattice</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hasse diagram with bottom a, top e, chain a-b-c-e on the left and a-d-e on the right; every pair has a unique join and meet, so it is already a lattice.

given.aim: Decide how many ordered pairs must be added to R so that every pair of elements in X gains a unique join and meet.

terms.example: Elements arranged with a lowest and e highest
terms.connects: The carrier set whose pairs must all have join and meet

terms.meaning: The given reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive relation (partial order)
terms.example: Includes a < b < c < e and a < d < e
terms.connects: The relation we may extend by adding ordered pairs

terms.meaning: Least and greatest elements of (X, R)
terms.example: a R x and x R e for every x
terms.connects: Their presence guarantees common bounds for every pair

terms.meaning: Pairs not linked by the order whose join/meet must be checked
terms.example: b and d are side by side, neither below the other
terms.connects: If each has a unique LUB and GLB, no pair needs fixing

given.plan: List the incomparable pairs, compute their join and meet, and confirm each is unique; if so the poset is already a lattice and the number of pairs to add is 0.

steps.title: Read the order and isolate the incomparable pairs
steps.formula_raw: lattice <=> every pair has a unique join (LUB) and unique meet (GLB)
steps.apply: From R the strict relations are a < b < c < e and a < d < e, with a the bottom and e the top. Comparable pairs trivially have join = larger and meet = smaller element. The only incomparable pairs are {b, d}, {c, d} (b vs d and c vs d), so these are the ones to verify.
steps.note: A bottom a and top e already ensure every pair has at least one common upper and lower bound.

steps.title: Check uniqueness of join and meet for each incomparable pair
steps.formula_raw: b ^ d = a, b v d = e ; c ^ d = a, c v d = e  (each unique)
steps.apply: For {b, d}: common lower bounds = {a} so meet = a (unique); common upper bounds = {e} so join = e (unique). For {c, d}: common lower bounds = {a} so meet = a (unique); common upper bounds = {e} so join = e (unique). Every pair therefore has a unique LUB and GLB, so (X, R) is already a lattice. No ordered pairs need to be added: the minimum is 0.
steps.note: Because both a and e are present and the two side branches meet only at a and e, there is never a tie for join or meet.

solution.result: Every pair already has a unique join and meet, so (X, R) is already a lattice; the minimum number of ordered pairs to add is 0.

questions.formula_note: With a as bottom and e as top, the only incomparable pairs {b, d} and {c, d} each have a unique meet (a) and join (e), so the poset is already a lattice and 0 pairs are needed.

questions.question: Consider the following Hasse diagrams. (i) a 4-element diamond: a bottom, two incomparable middle elements, and a top. (ii) a 5-element diagram in which two elements at the upper level share two common lower covers (a non-unique meet/join shape). (iii) a 6-element diagram with crossing edges in which a pair of elements has two incomparable upper bounds (and a pair has two incomparable lower bounds). (iv) a 5-element pentagon (N5): a bottom, a top, one side a 2-element chain and the other side a single element. Which all of the above represent a lattice? A. (i) and (iv) only B. (ii) and (iii) only C. (iii) only D. (i), (ii) and (iv) only
questions.to_find: Which of the four Hasse diagrams are lattices

understand.plain: A diagram is a lattice exactly when every pair of elements has a UNIQUE least upper bound (join) and a UNIQUE greatest lower bound (meet). Diagram (i) is the 4-element diamond: the two middle elements meet at the bottom and join at the top, both unique, so it IS a lattice. Diagram (iv) is the pentagon N5: it has a single top and single bottom and every pair resolves to a unique join and meet (N5 is a lattice, just non-modular), so it IS a lattice. Diagrams (ii) and (iii) each contain a pair of elements with TWO minimal upper bounds (or two maximal lower bounds), so the join (or meet) is not unique and they are NOT lattices. Hence only (i) and (iv) qualify: option A.

keywords.explain: Check that every pair of elements has a unique join (LUB) and a unique meet (GLB); a single pair with two incomparable upper or lower bounds breaks the lattice property.
keywords.example: In (iii) two elements have two incomparable upper bounds, so their join does not exist.

keywords.explain: The 4-element diamond and the 5-element pentagon are both genuine lattices (each pair has unique join and meet), even though they are not distributive/modular in general.
keywords.example: (i) diamond and (iv) pentagon both pass the test.

keywords.explain: A poset where some pair has more than one minimal upper bound (no unique join) or more than one maximal lower bound (no unique meet).
keywords.example: (ii) and (iii) fail because a join or meet is ambiguous.

understand.visual_alt: Four Hasse diagrams: the diamond (i) and pentagon (iv) are lattices with unique joins and meets, while (ii) and (iii) have a pair with two incomparable bounds and are not lattices, so the answer is A.

given.aim: Determine which of the four Hasse diagrams satisfy the lattice property and select the matching option.

terms.meaning: 4-element poset: bottom, two incomparable middle elements, top
terms.example: Middle pair meets at bottom, joins at top, both unique

terms.meaning: 5-element poset where a pair has two minimal upper bounds (or maximal lower bounds)
terms.example: An element pair lacks a unique join

terms.meaning: 6-element poset with crossing edges so a pair has two incomparable upper bounds
terms.example: Join of that pair is not unique

terms.meaning: 5-element poset with single top and bottom, one side a 2-chain
terms.example: Every pair has a unique join and meet

given.plan: Apply the lattice test (unique join and unique meet for every pair) to each diagram; (i) and (iv) pass while (ii) and (iii) have a pair with two incomparable bounds and fail, giving option A.

steps.title: State and apply the lattice criterion
steps.formula_raw: lattice <=> every pair {x, y} has a UNIQUE LUB (x v y) and a UNIQUE GLB (x ^ y)
steps.apply: Scan each diagram for any pair of elements that has two incomparable minimal upper bounds (then the join is undefined) or two incomparable maximal lower bounds (then the meet is undefined). One such pair is enough to disqualify a diagram.
steps.note: Having a single top and single bottom is necessary but not sufficient; internal pairs must also resolve uniquely.

steps.title: Classify the four diagrams
steps.formula_raw: (i) diamond: unique join/meet -> lattice ; (iv) pentagon N5: unique join/meet -> lattice ; (ii),(iii): a pair has two incomparable bounds -> not a lattice
steps.apply: (i) The diamond's two middle elements meet uniquely at the bottom and join uniquely at the top: lattice. (iv) The pentagon N5 has a single top and bottom and every pair has a unique join and meet (it is a lattice, just non-modular): lattice. (ii) and (iii) each contain a pair whose upper (or lower) bounds are two incomparable elements, so the join (or meet) is not unique: NOT lattices. Therefore exactly (i) and (iv) are lattices, which is option A.
steps.note: Crossing edges in (iii) and the shared covers in (ii) are visual cues that a join or meet is ambiguous.

solution.result: Only the diamond (i) and the pentagon (iv) have a unique join and meet for every pair, so the lattices are (i) and (iv) only: option A.

questions.formula_note: Each diagram is tested by the lattice definition (unique join and meet for every pair); the diamond (i) and pentagon (iv) pass while (ii) and (iii) fail because some pair has two incomparable bounds, giving A.

formulas.name: Lattice (Unique Join and Meet)
formulas.plain: a partially ordered set is a lattice iff every pair of elements has a unique least upper bound (join, x v y) and a unique greatest lower bound (meet, x ^ y)
formulas.whenToUse: Use to test whether a poset / Hasse diagram is a lattice, or to compute joins and meets; a single pair with two incomparable upper or lower bounds means it is NOT a lattice.

terms.means: join: the unique least upper bound of x and y
meet: the unique greatest lower bound of x and y
there exists exactly one (uniqueness is required)

formulas.trap: Having a single top and bottom element is not enough; some internal pair can still have two incomparable minimal upper bounds (or maximal lower bounds), breaking uniqueness of the join (or meet).
formulas.reference: Partial orders and lattices, set theory & algebra

formulas.name: Distributive Law for Lattices
formulas.plain: a lattice is distributive when x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z) holds for all x, y, z (equivalently the dual x ^ (y v z) = (x ^ y) v (x ^ z))
formulas.whenToUse: Use to test distributivity of a lattice; the diamond M3 and pentagon N5 are the two minimal lattices that violate this law (M5/M3 fails distributivity, N5 fails modularity).

terms.means: arbitrary lattice elements; the identity must hold for every choice

formulas.trap: The law must hold for EVERY triple; a single counterexample (as in M3 with three incomparable atoms) makes the lattice non-distributive, even though most triples still satisfy it.
formulas.reference: Distributive lattices, M3-N5 theorem, set theory & algebra
